---
title: "Water You May Not Sell: The Transferability of Groundwater Use Rights and Allocations Under Nebraska Law"
subtitle: "Ownership, Natural Resources District Authority, and the Limits of a Private Market"
author:
  - name: "Robert C. Cline, Sr., CPL"
    affiliations:
      - name: "Golden Triangle Land Services, Inc."
date: today
abstract: |
  Nebraska irrigators, lenders, and land brokers increasingly ask a deceptively
  simple question: can the right to pump groundwater—or an allocated pumping
  allowance measured in acre-inches or certified irrigated acres—be sold, leased,
  or moved from one owner or parcel to another? This Article answers that question.
  It locates the answer in three layers of Nebraska law that must be read together:
  (1) the common-law characterization of the groundwater right as a *usufructuary*
  right appurtenant to the overlying land, not ownership of the corpus of the water;
  (2) the Nebraska Ground Water Management and Protection Act, which superimposes a
  public-management regime administered by locally elected Natural Resources
  Districts (NRDs); and (3) the state and federal constitutional constraints that
  cabin both the landowner's right and the State's regulatory reach. The Article
  concludes that transfers of certified irrigated acres, certified water uses, and
  allocations are legally possible but are neither free nor freely alienable: they
  occur only through, and on the conditions imposed by, the NRD in which the water
  well is located, and only where a management area has been designated. A private
  brokerage market can facilitate such transfers but cannot manufacture a
  commodity—severable, freely tradable groundwater divorced from land and from NRD
  approval—that Nebraska law has never recognized.
bibliography: bibliography.bib
csl: BluebookDSEStyle.csl
suppress-bibliography: true
notes-after-punctuation: true
link-citations: true
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    theme: cosmo
  pdf:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    documentclass: article
    geometry: margin=1in
    fontsize: 12pt
  docx:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
---

*© 2026 Robert C. Cline, Sr., CPL. All rights reserved. Pre-publication draft — please do not
cite or circulate without the author's permission.*

<!-- DRAFTING NOTE (delete before circulation): This is an AI-assisted working
draft. Every case and statute cite was verified against a primary source during
drafting (see CITATION_AUDIT.md), but pinpoint page numbers and the current text
of amended statutes must be confirmed against Westlaw/Lexis and the current
Nebraska Revised Statutes before publication. In a Zotero/Word workflow, run
Refresh before finalizing so the Epps CSL's Rule 10.9 short-form logic settles. -->

# Introduction {#sec-intro}

Groundwater sustains Nebraska agriculture. The State overlies the High Plains
(Ogallala) Aquifer and reports on the order of nine million acres under
groundwater irrigation.[^acres] As aquifers decline in places, as interstate
compact obligations tighten, and as land changes hands, a recurring transactional
question has moved from the margins to the center of Nebraska land practice: **may
the right to use groundwater, or an allocated pumping allowance, be transferred,
assigned, leased, or otherwise conveyed from one landowner to another—and if so,
under what statutory and regulatory conditions?**

[^acres]: Nebraska's Natural Resources Districts report roughly 9.06 million
groundwater-irrigated acres statewide, compiled under Neb. Rev. Stat. § 46-739.
The figure varies year to year and between *certified* and *actually irrigated*
acres.

The answer requires assembling three bodies of law that Nebraska courts and the
Legislature have historically kept in separate drawers. First is the common law of
groundwater ownership, which characterizes what a landowner actually holds.
Second is the Nebraska Ground Water Management and Protection Act (the "Act" or
"GWMPA"), the statutory regime that delegates day-to-day groundwater management to
locally elected Natural Resources Districts. Third is the constitutional
framework—both the Nebraska Constitution's silence on groundwater and the federal
dormant Commerce Clause—which sets the outer bounds of both the private right and
the public power to regulate it.

This Article proceeds in the order of the questions a careful practitioner would
ask. Part [-@sec-ownership] establishes *who owns groundwater* in Nebraska and
concludes that the landowner holds a usufructuary right, not ownership in place.
Part [-@sec-statute] describes the statutory framework. Part [-@sec-nrd] situates
the NRDs within it—their constitutional footing, delegated powers, and limits.
Part [-@sec-allocation] explains how allocations, certifications, and irrigated-acre
determinations are established. Part [-@sec-transfer] answers the central
question—transferability along every axis (parcel, owner, well, district;
temporary and permanent). Part [-@sec-approvals] catalogs the approvals a transfer
requires. Part [-@sec-local] documents how individual NRDs differ. Part
[-@sec-market] asks whether a private brokerage market could lawfully exist.

# Who Owns Groundwater in Nebraska? {#sec-ownership}

## Not ownership in place: the rejection of the English rule

Nebraska has never recognized absolute ownership of groundwater in the overlying
landowner. The foundational case is *Olson v. City of Wahoo*, decided in 1933, in
which the Nebraska Supreme Court expressly rejected the English rule of absolute
ownership and adopted instead a modified version of the American "reasonable use"
rule.[^olson] Under that rule, the overlying owner may appropriate subterranean
water for reasonable and beneficial use on the overlying land, but not "in excess
of a reasonable and beneficial use," and—critically—"if the natural underground
supply is insufficient for all owners, each is entitled to a reasonable proportion
of the whole."[^olsonquote] That last clause grafts a *correlative-rights* limit
onto the American rule; commentators and later courts have called the resulting
hybrid the "Nebraska rule of reasonable use."[^nebrule]

[^olson]: *See* @olson1933, 811 (rejecting the English rule of absolute ownership
and adopting a modified American reasonable-use rule). The English (absolute
ownership) and California (correlative rights) rules are canvassed in @prather1978.

[^olsonquote]: @olson1933, 811 (Neb. Rev. Stat. citation to the official reporter;
parallel cite 248 N.W. 304, 308).

[^nebrule]: *See* @harnsberger1963problems; @harnsberger1973windmills, 179
(tracing the doctrinal development); *see also* @aiken1980administration.

The correlative-rights component descends from the California doctrine first
articulated in *Katz v. Walkinshaw*.[^katz] Under a pure correlative-rights regime,
"the overlying landowners have no proprietary interest in the water," and in times
of shortage each shares proportionally.[^prather] Nebraska's rule blends the
American and correlative-rights doctrines and, as the Supreme Court reaffirmed in
*Prather v. Eisenmann*, must be "construed . . . in the light of" the statutory
domestic-use preference.[^prather2]

[^katz]: @katz1902 (origin of the correlative-rights doctrine).

[^prather]: @prather1978, *at* 770 (describing correlative rights).

[^prather2]: @prather1978, *at* 771.

## A usufructuary right appurtenant to the land

The practical upshot—and the pivot on which the transferability question turns—is
that the Nebraska landowner holds a *right to use*, not title to the water itself.
The Nebraska Supreme Court put the point bluntly in the decision reviewed by the
United States Supreme Court in *Sporhase*: Nebraska "does not recognize an absolute
ownership interest in ground water, but grants landowners only a right to use
ground water on the land from which it has been extracted."[^douglasquote] The
Court added that, municipal supply arrangements aside, "Nebraska forbids any
transportation of ground water off the land owned or controlled by the person who
has appropriated the water from its subterranean source."[^douglasquote2]

[^douglasquote]: @douglasSporhase1981, 706 (parallel cite 305 N.W.2d 614, 617);
*accord* @sporhase1982, 951–52.

[^douglasquote2]: @douglasSporhase1981, 710 (parallel cite 305 N.W.2d 614, 619).
This common-law prohibition on off-tract transport is the doctrinal baseline
against which every statutory transfer mechanism discussed in Part [-@sec-transfer]
must be measured.

The Legislature codified the public-ownership characterization in the GWMPA, and
the Supreme Court has since restated it as settled: "Ground water is owned by the
public, and the only right held by an overlying landowner is in the use of the
ground water."[^bamfordown] The use right, moreover, is *appurtenant* to the
overlying land: it does not exist in the abstract but arises from and depends on
ownership of that land, and cannot be severed from the land and held on its
own.[^derivative]

[^bamfordown]: @bamford1994. The Nebraska DNR's official GWMPA compilation
attributes this proposition to *Bamford* in the annotations to Neb. Rev. Stat.
§ 46-702.

[^derivative]: That the use right is appurtenant to, and derivative of, ownership of
the overlying land follows from settled authority: the reasonable-use rule confines
the right to use on the overlying land, @olson1933, 811, and the Nebraska Supreme
Court has described the interest as a right to use ground water only on the land
from which it is extracted, @douglasSporhase1981, 706. *See also* @bamford1994
(ground water is publicly owned; the landowner holds only a use right).

That characterization matters enormously. If groundwater were owned in place, it
would be freely severable and salable like minerals or timber. Because it is only a
usufructuary, land-appurtenant, publicly owned resource, its "transfer" is not a
conveyance of a chattel but a regulated reallocation of a *use privilege*—one the
State, through the NRDs, may condition or deny.

# The Statutory Framework {#sec-statute}

## Two systems, separately administered

Nebraska administers surface water and groundwater through two distinct systems.
Surface water is governed by prior appropriation—first in time, first in
right—administered by the Department of Natural Resources under a constitutional
dedication of "the water of every natural stream" to the people for beneficial
use.[^constsurface] Groundwater is governed by the common-law reasonable-use rule
as modified and administered under the GWMPA by the NRDs.[^twoSystems] The
Nebraska Constitution, notably, *does not* address groundwater; its water
provisions speak to natural streams.[^constGW]

[^constsurface]: Neb. Const. art. XV, §§ 4–6 (declaring water a "natural want,"
dedicating the use of every natural stream to the people, and adopting priority of
appropriation for surface water).

[^twoSystems]: The two-system structure is described in @spearT2005, 181–84. The
GWMPA appears at Neb. Rev. Stat. §§ 46-701 to 46-755.

[^constGW]: Neb. Const. art. XV, §§ 4–7 (addressing surface water only); *see*
@spearT2005 (noting the Nebraska Constitution does not address groundwater use).

## From common law to comprehensive management

The GWMPA is the product of an evolutionary statutory history. The Legislature
first assigned groundwater regulatory authority to the NRDs in the 1975 Ground
Water Management Act;[^lb577] required groundwater management plans in
1985;[^lb1106] and, in a series of amendments culminating in 2004, integrated the
management of hydrologically connected surface water and groundwater, most
importantly through the integrated-management-plan and fully-appropriated-basin
provisions.[^integration] The 2004 integration statutes were enacted against the
backdrop of, and partly in response to, escalating Republican River Compact
litigation with Kansas.[^compact]

[^lb577]: Laws 1975, LB 577 (originating the Ground Water Management Act and
assigning regulatory authority to NRDs), codified as amended at Neb. Rev. Stat.
§ 46-701 et seq.

[^lb1106]: Laws 1985, LB 1106 (requiring NRDs to adopt groundwater management
plans for quantity and quality).

[^integration]: Neb. Rev. Stat. §§ 46-713 to 46-719 (integrated management plans;
determinations that a basin, subbasin, or reach is fully or overappropriated;
consequent stays on new wells and increases in irrigated acres). *See generally*
@aiken2004tributary.

[^compact]: Republican River Compact, ch. 104, 57 Stat. 86 (1943); *see* @kansasNebraska2015
(enforcing the Compact against Nebraska and describing Nebraska's post-2007
integrated-management "regulatory backstop," codified at Neb. Rev. Stat. § 46-715).

## *Spear T Ranch* and the tort interface

Where groundwater and surface water are hydrologically connected, the Supreme
Court supplied a common-law rule of decision in *Spear T Ranch, Inc. v. Knaub*,
adopting Restatement (Second) of Torts §§ 858 and 850A to govern conflicts between
users of hydrologically connected surface water and groundwater.[^spearT] *Spear T*
is not a transfer case, but it is indispensable background: it confirms that the
groundwater use right is bounded by a reasonableness standard enforceable in tort,
and that a "transfer" cannot enlarge a right beyond what reasonableness and the
Act's integrated-management provisions permit.[^spearT2]

[^spearT]: @spearT2005, 194 (adopting Restatement (Second) of Torts §§ 858, 850A
(1979)); *see* @restatement2dTorts.

[^spearT2]: The subsequent appeal, @spearT2006, addressed intervention and
reaffirmed the case-by-case reasonableness inquiry.

# Natural Resources Districts Within the Framework {#sec-nrd}

## Origin and constitutional footing

Nebraska's 23 NRDs are the institutional core of groundwater management. They were
created by the merger of some 150 special-purpose districts under Legislative Bill
1357, enacted in 1969 and effective July 1, 1972.[^lb1357] Organized along
river-basin lines and governed by locally elected boards, they are multipurpose
subdivisions charged with soil and water conservation, flood control, and—since
1975—groundwater management.[^nrdpowers]

[^lb1357]: Laws 1969, LB 1357, codified as amended at Neb. Rev. Stat. § 2-3201 et
seq.; *see* @neeman1974 (describing the consolidation and upholding the enabling
legislation).

[^nrdpowers]: Neb. Rev. Stat. § 2-3201 (legislative intent); *id.* § 46-707 (NRD
groundwater powers enumerated).

There is no *constitutional* groundwater provision that either authorizes or
constrains the NRDs; the constitutional water articles concern surface
water.[^noconstgw] The NRDs' authority is therefore statutory, resting on the
Legislature's police power to regulate use of a publicly owned resource. The
Nebraska Supreme Court upheld the NRD enabling legislation against constitutional
attack in *Neeman v. Nebraska Natural Resources Commission*,[^neeman] and has
repeatedly confirmed that limitations on groundwater withdrawal are a valid
exercise of the police power that do not, without more, effect a compensable
taking.[^bamfordtaking]

[^noconstgw]: *See supra* notes on Neb. Const. art. XV.

[^neeman]: @neeman1974 (rejecting single-subject and improper-amendment challenges;
holding the NRD statutes constitutional).

[^bamfordtaking]: @bamford1994 (limitations on groundwater withdrawals during
shortage are a valid exercise of the police power and do not necessarily constitute
a taking requiring compensation).

## Delegated powers and their limits

Section 46-707 enumerates the NRD's groundwater powers, and § 46-739 supplies the
menu of "controls" an NRD may adopt once it has designated a ground water
*management area*.[^controls] These include allocation, well-spacing and metering
requirements, rotation, limits on or reductions of certified irrigated acres, and
limits on increases in consumptive use.[^controls2] The delegation is real but
bounded in at least four ways. First, most controls require a designated management
area and the procedural predicates (data, plan, hearing, order) the Act
specifies.[^predicate] Second, § 46-739 itself commands that any transfer rules an
NRD adopts must *deny or condition* transfers where necessary to protect other
groundwater users and surface water appropriators, to protect compact compliance,
and to protect the public interest.[^statlimits] Third, the DNR retains a
supervisory and integrating role for hydrologically connected basins.[^dnrrole]
Fourth, the NRD's standing to act is itself limited: it may not, for example,
litigate the "public interest" at large absent a district-specific
interest.[^twinPlatte]

[^controls]: Neb. Rev. Stat. § 46-739(1) (controls available in a designated
management area).

[^controls2]: *Id.* § 46-739(1)(a)–(m); *id.* § 46-740 (allocation and reduction of
irrigated acres).

[^predicate]: *Id.* §§ 46-707, 46-739; *see id.* § 46-743 (notice and hearing
requirements).

[^statlimits]: *Id.* § 46-739(1)(k) (requiring that transfer rules deny or condition
a transfer to (A) ensure consistency with the management-area purpose, (B) prevent
adverse effects on other groundwater users or surface water appropriators, (C)
prevent adverse effects on interstate-compact or decree compliance, and (D)
otherwise protect the public interest and prevent detriment to the public welfare).

[^dnrrole]: *Id.* §§ 46-713 to 46-719 (integrated management; DNR determinations of
appropriation status).

[^twinPlatte]: @twinPlatte1996 (the NRD enabling statute does not confer standing to
represent the public interest in litigation where the district lacks an independent
basis for standing).

# How Groundwater Allocations Are Established {#sec-allocation}

Before a right can be transferred, it must be defined. Nebraska law defines the
groundwater use interest through a stack of administrative determinations.

**Well registration.** Every non-exempt water well must be registered with the
Department; registration is the baseline record of who is pumping, from where, and
for what.[^wellreg]

**Well construction permits.** In fully or overappropriated areas and in many
management areas, a well may not be constructed without an NRD permit, and permits
may be denied or conditioned during a stay on new wells.[^wellpermit]

**Certified irrigated acres.** The operative unit for most agricultural transfers is
not a volume of water but the *certified irrigated acre*—a specific acre certified,
under NRD rules, as historically irrigated from a given water source.[^certacres]
Certification typically rests on Farm Service Agency records, aerial imagery, and,
where those are unavailable, county records or sworn testimony.[^certproof]

**Allocation.** Where an NRD imposes quantity controls, it may allocate a permitted
withdrawal—commonly expressed in acre-inches per certified irrigated acre over a
multi-year allocation period—and may require reductions in certified irrigated
acres.[^allocation]

**Integrated management.** In hydrologically connected and fully/overappropriated
basins, the certified acres and allocations sit inside an integrated management
plan jointly implemented by the NRD and the DNR, which caps new appropriations and
new irrigated acres.[^imp]

[^wellreg]: Neb. Rev. Stat. §§ 46-602 to 46-604 (water-well registration).

[^wellpermit]: *Id.* §§ 46-735 to 46-738 (well-construction permits); *id.* § 46-714
(stays on new well permits and on increases in groundwater-irrigated acres following
a fully-appropriated determination, and exceptions thereto).

[^certacres]: *Id.* § 46-739 (certified irrigated acres and certified water uses);
*see, e.g.*, the certification and transfer definitions in NRD rules cited *infra*
Part [-@sec-local].

[^certproof]: NRD certification rules commonly accept FSA Form 578 records and
aerial photography, with fallback to county records or sworn testimony (*e.g.*,
Central Platte NRD Rules & Regulations).

[^allocation]: Neb. Rev. Stat. § 46-740 (allocation; reduction of irrigated acres);
*id.* § 46-739(1)(e)–(f).

[^imp]: *Id.* §§ 46-713 to 46-719.

# May Groundwater Allocations Be Transferred? {#sec-transfer}

With the pieces defined, the central question can be answered directly: **yes,
certified irrigated acres, certified water uses, and allocations may be
transferred—but only through the NRD, only where the NRD's rules authorize it, and
only on the statutory and regulatory conditions below.** The common-law default,
recall, is *against* off-tract transfer;[^default] the statutory transfer
mechanisms are exceptions the Legislature and the NRDs have carved out of that
default.

[^default]: @douglasSporhase1981, 710 (off-tract transport historically forbidden
except for municipal supply).

## The statutory hook: § 46-739 transfers

The affirmative authority to approve transfers is found in § 46-739. Within a
designated management area, an NRD "may" adopt rules governing the transfer of
certified water uses and certified irrigated acres.[^739transfer] If it adopts such
rules, the statute *mandates* that they require the district to deny or condition
any transfer to the extent necessary to protect the management-area purpose, other
groundwater users, surface water appropriators, interstate-compact compliance, and
the public interest.[^739conditions] Transfers are thus permissive at the district
level (an NRD need not allow them) but, where allowed, are heavily conditioned by
statute.

[^739transfer]: Neb. Rev. Stat. § 46-739(1)(k) (authorizing NRD rules for transfers
of certified water uses and certified irrigated acres).

[^739conditions]: *Id.* § 46-739(1)(k)(A)–(D).

## Transfers along each axis

*Between parcels and between wells.* The typical transaction moves certified
irrigated acres from one tract (or well) to another within the same NRD—for
example, retiring irrigation on marginal ground and adding it to a more productive
field, or shifting acres between wells for efficiency. Because the right is
appurtenant to land and defined by certification, this is a *re-certification*
administered by the NRD, not a private conveyance of water.[^parcel]

*Between owners.* Certified acres and allocations commonly change hands with the
land in an ordinary sale; the use right runs with the overlying land. A transfer to
a *different* owner's separate tract, however, is a transfer in the § 46-739 sense
and requires NRD approval, the title report, and lienholder consent discussed in
Part [-@sec-approvals].[^owner]

*Between NRDs / interbasin.* Because each NRD adopts its own rules and each basin
has its own appropriation status and (where applicable) compact obligations, a
transfer crossing NRD lines must satisfy the rules of *both* districts and may be
barred outright where it would impair compact compliance.[^interNRD]

*Temporary versus permanent.* Nebraska's statutes contemplate both. Permanent
reallocation runs through the § 46-739 certified-acre/allocation transfer
machinery. Temporary or use-specific movements appear in narrower statutory
vehicles—for example, the § 46-691.03 permit to withdraw and transport groundwater
*off* the overlying land to augment streamflow, wetlands, or wildlife
habitat.[^temp] Some NRDs also operate "water banks" that credit and debit
consumptive use, a functionally temporary transfer device operating under district
rules.[^waterbank]

[^parcel]: Neb. Rev. Stat. § 46-739(1)(k); *see also id.* § 46-739.01 (procedural
conditions).

[^owner]: *Id.* § 46-739.01.

[^interNRD]: *Id.* § 46-739(1)(k)(C) (compact-protection condition); *see* @kansasNebraska2015
(illustrating the stakes of compact compliance in the Republican River basin).

[^temp]: *Id.* § 46-691.03 (permit to withdraw and transport groundwater off the
overlying land for streamflow, wetland, or wildlife benefit, requiring compliance
with the rules of both the source and destination NRDs).

[^waterbank]: *See, e.g.*, Central Platte NRD Rules & Regulations (water-bank
crediting of consumptive use), discussed *infra* Part [-@sec-local].

## The interstate dimension

Transfer *out of Nebraska* is governed by § 46-613.01 and by the constitutional
gloss of *Sporhase*. The statute conditions an interstate transport permit on
findings that the withdrawal is reasonable, consistent with conservation, and not
detrimental to the public welfare—conditions the United States Supreme Court
sustained—plus a reciprocity requirement the Court struck down as an impermissible
burden on interstate commerce.[^sporhase] *Sporhase* holds that groundwater *is* an
article of commerce for Commerce Clause purposes, so Nebraska cannot flatly bar
export; but it may still regulate interstate transfer under conservation-based,
evenhanded criteria.[^sporhase2] The practical result is a narrow, permit-gated
channel for export, not a free market across state lines.

[^sporhase]: @sporhase1982, 954–58 (sustaining the first three permit conditions;
invalidating the reciprocity requirement); Neb. Rev. Stat. § 46-613.01.

[^sporhase2]: @sporhase1982, 953–54 (groundwater is an article of commerce);
*id.* at 956 (a State severely restricting its own citizens' withdrawals is not
discriminating against interstate commerce when it controls export).

# What Approvals Are Required? {#sec-approvals}

A compliant transfer of certified irrigated acres or certified water uses assembles
the following:

1. **NRD approval.** The transfer must be authorized by, and approved under, the
   rules of the NRD in which the water well is located (and, for interdistrict
   transfers, the destination NRD as well).[^app1]

2. **Statutory conditions satisfied.** The NRD must deny or condition the transfer
   as necessary to protect the management-area purpose, other groundwater users,
   surface water appropriators, compact compliance, and the public
   interest.[^app2]

3. **Report of title and lienholder consent.** Section 46-739.01 requires the
   applicant to submit a title report identifying the owner and all consensual
   liens on the source land, and to obtain written consent from each lienholder
   before the transfer may be approved; a lienholder whose interest is not shown
   and consented to retains a right of action.[^app3] Narrow exemptions exist for
   *de minimis* transfers (four acres or less) and for single-tract,
   single-landowner efficiency transfers with no net change in certified
   acres.[^app4]

4. **Notice and hearing.** Where the Act or the NRD's rules require, the transfer
   or the underlying rule change proceeds on published notice and public
   hearing.[^app5]

5. **DNR involvement for connected/appropriated basins.** In fully or
   overappropriated and hydrologically connected basins, the transfer operates
   within the integrated management plan, implicating DNR review.[^app6]

6. **Interstate permit.** Export requires the § 46-613.01 permit, subject to the
   *Sporhase*-surviving criteria.[^app7]

Recording of the transfer occurs at the district level through re-certification and
the district's transfer-permit records; the § 46-739.01 title-report and
lienholder-consent machinery supplies the real-property recording interface that
protects secured lenders.[^recording]

[^app1]: Neb. Rev. Stat. § 46-739(1)(k); *id.* § 46-691.03(4) (compliance with the
rules of both source and destination NRDs for off-tract transport).

[^app2]: *Id.* § 46-739(1)(k)(A)–(D).

[^app3]: *Id.* § 46-739.01(1)–(2).

[^app4]: *Id.* § 46-739.01(1) (exemptions for one-time transfers of four acres or
less, qualifying incentive-program transfers of four acres or less, and
single-landowner single-tract efficiency transfers with no net change).

[^app5]: *Id.* §§ 46-743, 46-739.

[^app6]: *Id.* §§ 46-713 to 46-719.

[^app7]: *Id.* § 46-613.01; @sporhase1982.

[^recording]: Neb. Rev. Stat. § 46-739.01(2) (preserving unlisted lienholders'
remedies).

# How Do Individual NRDs Differ? {#sec-local}

Nebraska groundwater law is *not* uniform statewide in operation, and by design.
The Act supplies a common statutory ceiling and a mandatory floor of protective
conditions, but it delegates the choice and calibration of controls to each NRD and
expressly authorizes *different controls for different conditions*. Section
46-739(4) permits an NRD to adopt different provisions for different categories of
use or portions of a management area where uniform application would fail to carry
out the Act "in a reasonably effective and equitable manner."[^local1] The threshold
question—whether an NRD allows transfers at all—is itself a matter of local
discretion, because § 46-739 transfer authority is permissive.[^local2]

The consequences are visible in district rulebooks. Districts under Republican
River Compact pressure (the Upper, Middle, and Lower Republican NRDs) administer
some of the strictest allocation and transfer regimes, tightly tied to compact
accounting.[^republican] Others define bespoke instruments: the South Platte NRD's
rules, for instance, define an "Application for a Transfer Permit" to cover the
physical transfer of groundwater, changes or additions in type of use, the transfer
of certified irrigated acres, *and* the transfer of an allocation.[^southplatte]
The Central Platte NRD operates a water bank and small-area transfer exemptions
keyed to soil class.[^centralplatte] A transfer that is routine in one district may
be unavailable, or available only on different terms, in the next.

[^local1]: Neb. Rev. Stat. § 46-739(4) (authorizing differentiated controls where
varying hydrologic, geologic, soil, or use conditions make uniform controls
ineffective or inequitable).

[^local2]: *Id.* § 46-739(1) ("may" adopt controls); *id.* § 46-739(1)(k) ("may"
adopt transfer rules).

[^republican]: The Republican River basin NRDs' regimes are shaped by the Compact
and its enforcement. *See* @kansasNebraska2015.

[^southplatte]: South Platte NRD, Amended and Restated Districtwide Ground Water
Management Area Rules & Regulations (defining "Application for a Transfer Permit").

[^centralplatte]: Central Platte NRD Rules & Regulations (water-bank crediting;
small-area transfer exemptions by soil class).

# Could a Private Brokerage Market Legally Exist? {#sec-market}

The final question is whether Nebraska law would permit a genuine private
brokerage market in groundwater—brokers matching buyers and sellers of severable,
tradable water rights the way real-estate or commodity brokers do. The answer is a
qualified *no as to the commodity, yes as to facilitation.*

Three features of Nebraska law constrain any such market. First, there is *no
severable commodity to trade.* The interest is a usufructuary, land-appurtenant,
publicly owned use right, not a fee interest in a corpus of water. What can move is
a *certified irrigated acre* or *allocation*, and only through
re-certification.[^market1] Second, *every transfer is gated by the NRD.* Approval,
statutory conditions, title report, and lienholder consent are prerequisites the
parties cannot contract around; a broker cannot deliver what the district has not
approved.[^market2] Third, the *public-ownership and conservation constraints*
recognized in *Sporhase* mean the State may regulate, condition, and in
appropriate circumstances deny transfers to protect the resource, other users, and
compact obligations—limits that a private market cannot displace.[^market3]

[^market1]: *See supra* Part [-@sec-ownership]; @douglasSporhase1981, 706–07, 710.

[^market2]: Neb. Rev. Stat. §§ 46-739, 46-739.01.

[^market3]: @sporhase1982, 953–58 (groundwater is an article of commerce but the
State may impose evenhanded, conservation-based restrictions); *see also*
@bamford1994 (withdrawal limits are a valid police-power exercise).

None of this forecloses market-*like* activity. Within a permissive NRD, private
parties may negotiate the price of a certified-acre transfer, and a broker or
consultant may lawfully identify counterparties, value the acres, and shepherd the
§ 46-739.01 title and lien work—much as brokers facilitate the transfer of
surface-water appropriations under the parallel surface-water transfer statutes.
The instructive analogy is precisely that surface-water regime, where
appropriations are transferable but only through a regulated change process that
protects other appropriators and caps the transferred right at historic consumptive
use.[^surface] Nebraska groundwater sits one step further from a free market than
surface water: the interest is more tightly bound to the land, the administering
body is local and plural, and the resource is declared publicly owned. A broker can
therefore build a business *facilitating* NRD-approved transfers; what a broker
cannot do is create, or promise, a freely alienable water commodity that Nebraska
law has never recognized and that its constitutional and statutory structure
affirmatively resists.

[^surface]: *Cf.* Neb. Rev. Stat. § 46-290 (surface-water appropriation transfers,
capped at historic consumptive use and conditioned on protection of other
appropriators). The comparison illuminates how Nebraska regulates *any* water
reallocation: through a public change process, not private conveyance.

# Conclusion {#sec-conclusion}

Nebraska law permits the transfer of groundwater use rights and allocations, but
only in a form its doctrine can accommodate. Because the landowner holds a
usufructuary, land-appurtenant, publicly owned *right to use*—not ownership of the
water in place—what actually moves in a "groundwater transfer" is a certified
irrigated acre, a certified water use, or an allocation, reallocated through the
NRD under § 46-739 and § 46-739.01, subject to mandatory protective conditions,
title and lienholder requirements, and, for connected and appropriated basins, DNR
integration. NRDs differ by design, so transferability is local. Export is
permit-gated under *Sporhase*. A private brokerage market may facilitate these
NRD-approved reallocations, but it cannot conjure a freely tradable commodity out
of a use privilege the State owns and the districts administer. For the Nebraska
landowner, lender, or broker, the operative rule is therefore not "who owns the
water" but "what will the NRD approve, and on what conditions."  

The author, Robert C. Cline, Sr. is a Certified Professional Landman (CPL). The author is not licensed to practice law. This Article is legal scholarship and general commentary; it is not legal advice, does not address any particular person's circumstances, and creates no attorney-client relationship. Readers with specific questions should consult a licensed Nebraska attorney. © 2026 Robert C. Cline, Sr., CPL, all rights reserved. 

<!-- OPTIONAL COLOPHON — for the repository / SSRN / working-paper version only.
     For that version, uncomment the block below (remove the opening and closing
     comment markers that immediately wrap the Colophon heading). Leave it
     commented for the law review submission, where typesetting and citation
     tools are, by convention, not cited. -->



This Article was prepared in Quarto (https://quarto.org), an open-source
scientific and technical publishing system, with references managed in Zotero.
Bluebook-style citations were formatted using the Epps Bluebook CSL (*Bluebook
Style — Epps Version*), a fork by Daniel Epps of the community Bluebook CSL
originally authored by Bruce D'Arcus and Nancy Sims with contributions from
Patrick O'Brien, distributed under the Creative Commons Attribution–ShareAlike
3.0 License (CC BY-SA 3.0).
